data.title: Jiayin Group: Why It Could Go Lower
data.description:
Jiayin Group (JFIN), a fintech company from China, has moved higher in recent days after sliding for months. This, together with JFIN possessing a number of intriguing attributes, including being available at very low multiples, makes for a compelling combination. However, there is reason to believe the stock may not yet be done with moving lower, as the headwinds facing JFIN have probably yet to hit peak strength, something that argues for standing pat in the meantime.

Why the recent bounce could be just a temporary stop on the way lower

A previous article from September 2025 written by myself mentioned how JFIN needed a bounce in the stock because the absence of one would open the door to significantly lower prices, at least as far as the charts were concerned. Furthermore, while JFIN released what most would agree was a blowout report with its Q2 FY2025 report, it was all for naught because the results were overshadowed by the uncertainty of what lay ahead, which included the prospect of lower earnings in coming quarters. Accordingly, I decided to rate JFIN a hold in the article.

The chart above shows how JFIN’s failure to engineer a sustained bounce opened the door for much lower prices. A second attempt was required in October because the trendline was able to hold the stock aloft on the first attempted break below the trendline, but the stock eventually broke below the trendline that had been in effect ever since the stock started to rally, going back all the way to last year.

The decline in the stock continued until it hit a low of $6.32 per ADS on December 1, which means JFIN has lost roughly two-thirds of its market cap since the stock peaked at $19.23 per ADS in June 2025. JFIN, as a fintech company in China, was not alone in selling off during this time because the entire sector went down in China, but it is nonetheless worth noting how JFIN fell more than most in the fintech space.

Why the stock could have further to drop

The stock is off the lows after closing at $6.92 per ADS on December 9, but there are several reasons why the stock may not yet have hit bottom. For instance, the chart below shows how JFIN fell below the trend, but before this happened, the trend allowed for the stock to rally from a low of $4.00 in August 2024 to a high of $19.23 in June 2025. If we then assume JFIN has been retracing this move upwards with a move downwards following the June 2025 high, then it is worth noting how the stock is below the 76.4% Fibonacci retracement of $4.00 to $19.23, or $7.59.

Furthermore, not only has the stock traded below $7.59, but the bounce in the last few days was only able to go as high as $7.32, or $0.27 below $7.59. This could be interpreted as JFIN encountering resistance. If the road higher is blocked by resistance, then the path of least resistance is downwards, possibly towards the next potential support level. This next potential support level after the 76.4% Fibonacci retracement entails a complete retracement from $4.00 to $19.23, which suggests the stock could be heading to $4.00, or where the stock started its rally last year.

Valuations for JFIN are higher than other peers in the sector

There are other reasons why JFIN is probably going lower, besides the charts. Most Chinese fintech stocks have fallen, like JFIN, but even though the latter has declined more than the former in many instances, JFIN still looks like a worse option than competing alternatives. For instance, a number of competitors are valued at less than the net cash on the balance sheet.

On the other hand, the balance sheet of JFIN looks much less strong, with cash and cash equivalents of just $17.44M, partially offset by $5M of total debt. This is nowhere close to JFIN’s market cap of $369M, which means JFIN does not compare well to competitors, some of whom are trading at a market cap that values them at less than the net cash they have.

JFIN is available at less than book value with a book value of $11.78 per ADS, which means JFIN trades at a price-to-book of 0.59x, but again, there are others in the space available at an even lower multiple. It will be difficult for JFIN to be picked ahead of other alternatives that are available at less than net cash, or trading at much lower multiples, and if buyers do not step in to keep the stock afloat, that raises the odds of the stock drifting lower.

A major reason why JFIN has much less cash than others is because it was much less conservative in terms of spending the cash coming in during the good times, which includes spending RMB1.35B on a piece of commercial property in March 2025, a transaction that your truly questioned as detailed in an earlier article.

What is giving JFIN and the fintech space in China problems?

As mentioned earlier, the entire fintech sector in China has been hit for months. This selloff can be attributed to the intervention by financial regulators in China, which seeks to control risk in the financial sector. This includes being more selective with loans to borrowers, which impacts JFIN since its business model revolves around facilitating loans to prospective borrowers.

The Chinese regulators have been speaking up about the need to control financial risk in the sector, especially during the summer, which coincided with the decline in the stock of many fintech players in China, JFIN included. The latest rule changes by regulators went into effect in October, and the effect on JFIN was to lose another 30% or so of its market cap, on top of earlier losses in the year.

JFIN is feeling the impact of changes made by financial regulators

The prior chart shows how the stock continued to drop in the days following the release of the most recent, or Q3 FY2025 report on November 25. This is because the report showed evidence of JFIN being adversely impacted by tighter financial regulations when it comes to lending in China. The previous article, for instance, raised the possibility of lower earnings after the strong results in Q2 FY2025, and this is exactly what happened in the Q3 FY2025 report.

One of the reasons, if not the main one, why Chinese regulators felt the urge to step in was in all likelihood the pace of credit growth due to fintech players in China. It varies from company to company, but, in many instances, the capital used for these loans is not from the fintech companies themselves, but from other financial institutions they partner with, mostly banks. In theory, if these loans go bad, there is the risk of financial contagion, which is something regulators want to avoid.

Among the various measures taken by financial regulators in China is to drain liquidity in the sector by limiting the credit growth taking place, which in practice means a lower volume of loans facilitated since there is less access to capital needed. Another is to require that banks become more stringent with their exposure to risks emanating from working with fintech players, which means they should rely less on some of the tasks currently performed by fintech players, like risk assessment, and instead do their own. This means banks have to do more because they can rely less on fintech players, which means the latter can charge the former less in terms of fees and so on.

All this could greatly impact a company like JFIN and its business model, which primarily revolves around facilitating loans and the income generated from it. The Q3 FY2025 report suggests this is already happening because even though the numbers grew YoY, they shrank QoQ. For instance, non-GAAP income from operations reached RMB490.6M, or $68.9M, in Q3 FY2025, which resulted in net income of RMB376.5M, or $52.9M.

In comparison, these numbers are up from a year ago when JFIN reported RMB326.5M and RMB269.6M, respectively, in Q3 FY2024, but they are worse than in Q2 FY2025 when JFIN reported RMB737.6M and RMB519.1M, respectively. Per share, JFIN earned RMB7.32 or $1.04 per ADS in Q3 FY2025, up from RMB5.08 or $0.72 in Q3 FY2024, but down from RMB9.84 or $1.36 in Q2 FY2025.

Loan facilitation volume was RMB32.2B or $4.5B in Q3 FY2025, up from RMB26.7B or $3.8B in Q3 FY2024, but down from RMB37.1B or $5.2B in Q2 FY2025. Furthermore, this decrease in volume is expected to continue. JFIN actually lowered its prior outlook for loan volume in FY2025 with the updated one calling for loan facilitation volume of RMB127.8-129.8B in FY2025, which is a reduction of RMB9.2-12.2B compared to the prior outlook calling for RMB137.0-142.0B.

This implies a loan facilitation volume of RMB23.0-25.0B in Q4 FY2025 after subtracting volume in Q1-Q3, down from RMB27.7B in Q4 FY2024 and down from RMB32.2B in the preceding Q3 FY2025. In the span of two quarters, loan volume is expected to go from a high of RMB37.1B in Q2 FY2025 to RMB24B, the midpoint of RMB23-25B, which represents a reduction of 35.3%. The ninety-day+ delinquency ratio was 1.33%, which is not bad, but it is up 21 basis points QoQ. If the intent of Chinese regulators is to engineer a deleveraging in the fintech sector by intervening, then it seems to be working.

JFIN is quickly getting worse

Importantly, the outlook calls for non-GAAP income from operations of RMB1.99-2.06B in FY2025, which, at the midpoint, implies income from operations of about RMB190.11M in Q4 FY2025 since income from operations totaled RMB1,834.89M in Q1-Q3 FY2025. This is less than half the RMB402.4M income from operations achieved in Q4 FY2024.

It also marks an acceleration in the decline in earnings compared to Q2 FY2025 and Q3 FY2025 when income from operations was RMB737.6M and RMB490.6M, respectively. Using this, I estimate JFIN will earn about $0.36 per ADS in Q4 FY2025, or half the $0.72 or RMB5.20 earned in Q4 FY2024.

FY2025 earnings are projected to end up at $4.16 per ADS since EPADS in Q1, Q2 and Q3 FY2025 were $1.40, $1.36, and $1.04, respectively. This implies JFIN is trading at just 1.66x projected FY2025 earnings, which may seem like a super bargain, but keep in mind that this is with earnings going in the wrong direction and fast.

JFIN has not given any insights into next year or FY2026, and much of it is outside of its control since much depends on what the Chinese government does, but if earnings keep going down the way they have, JFIN might post a net loss next year, perhaps as soon as the first half. This also raises doubts about the safety of the dividend. JFIN paid an annual dividend of $0.80 per ADS in July, which implies a dividend yield of 11.6%, but it will be difficult for JFIN to sustain this dividend if earnings melt away like they have recently.

JFIN could be more sensitive to regulatory changes than others

As mentioned earlier, JFIN has fallen off more than others. There are several reasons for this. For instance, the balance sheet of JFIN has much less cash than others, which can be used to ride out and absorb headwinds in the short term. However, there seems to be another reason why JFIN could potentially be more sensitive to changes brought about by regulators.

The fintech space in China is covered by a fairly large number of companies, and while they all fall under the label of fintech as they do share some similarities as they all compete in the fintech market in China, they also differ in a number of areas. One of them is in terms of how each company has approached the use of artificial intelligence, or AI.

Fintech in China has embraced the use of AI and JFIN has been no exception, but JFIN has gone further than many others by using it in key areas like loan approval, unlike others that have been conservative by sticking to more mundane tasks like answering customer queries and customer service in general.

The issue here is that with the recently imposed changes, regulators are forcing financial institutions, including the ones that partner with companies like JFIN, to keep a close eye on potential risks that may previously have been overlooked or not taken as seriously. This could affect a company like JFIN, which has developed its own proprietary AI model that is used to approve loans.

On paper, this aspect of JFIN could make its partners more likely to reduce the liquidity that JFIN needs to facilitate loans since JFIN does not have that much capital on its own, unlike some of its competitors that actually use their own capital when they lend money to borrowers and have the necessary license to do so.

Investor takeaways

JFIN is now available at roughly a third of what it went for at the summer peak. The stock has also shown signs of a bounce after sliding for months. Combine this with the fact that JFIN trades at very low multiples, including a P/E in the low single digits, and it is not hard to see why JFIN may draw a look from potential buyers, especially those looking for a bargain.

However, earnings are going down fast, primarily as a result of changes brought about by financial regulators in China. JFIN could potentially earn an estimated $0.36 in the last quarter of FY2025, or almost a fourth of what it earned in the first quarter. This change of fortunes at JFIN is driven by intervention by regulators, which essentially seeks to control potential risks in the financial sector.

Regulators have sought to deleverage the sector by draining liquidity, which shows up in the drastic reduction in loan volume facilitated and the need for JFIN to lower its FY2025 outlook in the Q3 FY2025 report. Regulators have also forced financial institutions to pay greater attention to possible risks, which has in turn led to various consequences like lower fees since some of the functions like risk assessment fintech players were tasked with have to also be done internally. The net effect on JFIN is much lower profits.

JFIN also does not really have any control as to when it can reverse the ongoing contraction in loans facilitated because it depends on what the financial institutions it partners with do, which in turn are affected by what the financial regulators do. JFIN could be more sensitive to regulators because of the way it has embraced the use of AI, more than many others in the fintech space.

The balance sheet of JFIN also leaves it less room compared to others. There are competitors out there that come with significantly lower valuations, which includes being valued at less than the net cash on the books in some cases, even though their stock price has not fallen to the same extent as JFIN. This means it will not be easy for JFIN to sway potential buyers to its side, which it will need to do to sustain a rebound in the stock.

I am neutral on JFIN. The stock has dropped a lot in the past half year, but there is reason to believe it can go lower. Whether it is the quest of financial regulators to limit what could be potentially risky behavior in the sector, including when it comes to AI, the income statement and the balance sheet that are heading in the wrong direction with JFIN potentially in the red next year, or the charts, the possibility JFIN is heading to the $4.00 price point is there.

data.title: Maersk: Upside From The Q3'25 Period With A Guidance Increase
data.description:
My investment in Maersk (AMKBY) has now been in my portfolio for going on 2 years, with a decent return. Nothing market-beating yet - but still decent, and I expect it to outperform if it swings up further. Maersk has the potential to, in a relatively short period of time, see relatively significant upsides - examples of this can be seen over the past 20 years. It's kind of like the market, from time to time, realizes that the company's market position, only to then realize it's gone too far, and drive it down again. Between 2005 and 2025, the company's native highs and lows have been quite similar, with the obvious exception of the post-COVID-19 2020 period, when the company bounced up significantly.

But we're out of that now. What drives this company is the sort of freight and logistics trends that I've been describing as relevant for Maersk over the past few years.

The latest results for the company, which I will also be looking at here, are the 3Q25 results. These were, to give you a sneak peek, a very good set of results - and I will slightly bump my targets due to their performance. But I wouldn't go so far as to characterize this quarter or even this year as thesis-changing. We're still talking of what amounts to a slight change - not something that changes this company's fundamentals in any major way.

But there are positives, some that show us one of the best markets in over 14 years at this point - this is worth highlighting, and is something I will look at here.

It's also worth mentioning that the company's native yield is currently still close to 8% - while nowhere close to the record levels of a few years back, that's still a very solid yield and a very good upside beside the valuation (which at over 14,000 DKK is so-so).

My latest article on the company is here.

Maersk - Upside in shipping tells us we're having an upside not seen since 2011.

The logistics industry is expanding. Investors who generally are out of this market are, I believe, doing themselves a disservice by not at least considering the companies in it. It doesn't have to be Maersk either. I've been heavily invested in cheap cost bases in both UPS (UPS) and companies like DHL (DHLGY) for some time, all bought at cheap valuations - both of them are either up or flat. My position at Maersk is an addition to this.

Why are logistics such an interesting sector now?

No one can have missed out on the E-commerce boom. It's global, and it's created a demand for instant delivery (24 hours) - and the companies that can't fix this are usually not long for this market, at least for shorter-term deliveries. Maersk is obviously in a longer-term game. Since 2011, we've seen significant increases year-over-year, even if some years it's been more sluggish than others. The overall trend is positive.

It can be said, with fairness, that I went "HOLD" too early on Maersk. The simple fact is that Maersk has outperformed since my rating change. It goes in the vein of my biggest flaws as an investor (as I consider them) - I have a tendency to rate down too early, and to exit too early. It goes back to being "safe and conservative", and it's a bias I'm working on balancing a bit more.

3Q25 saw excellent execution across all of Maersk's major business lines. We're talking EBITDA of over $2.6B for the quarter, with revenue growth from L&S, as well as important margin improvement. The company's hub-and-spoke model, Gemini (not the AI), is driving strong cost savings, despite ongoing rate pressure for the company's shipping.

This is why the Gemini cooperation and model was created - to offset trends like this one. Both L&S and Terminals are, as you can see above, executing strongly - currently holding the company back a bit is Ocean, which remains the company's strongest and biggest segment.

Also a concern, and the reason I am perhaps a bit more conservative than other logistics investors insofar as Maersk goes (i like some of the land-based more in terms of valuation) is the reliance on China. The nation's export is still the major growth engine for volume, and if it weren't for China's growth, things would be significantly lower. Positive in this quarter, though - the company's segment growth from other parts was also increasing.

However, Far East Asia as a segment still accounts for almost 30% of the total volume and freight, which shows you how important China is to the company.

The company's guidance update is yet another positive update. With low-end guidance increasing to almost $1B better in EBITDA, it's a significant improvement. The company also now expects about one billion less (dollars) in CapEx for the full year 2025.

The second concern that I have is the company's continued buybacks. I don't see the value of the buybacks at this price - and as we know, buybacks may artificially support share prices - and I believe that's definitely part of what's happening here.

This, coupled with the risk of an oversupply of freight and a recovery of red sea shipping, which would result in further dropping freight rates, is the main risk to Maersk at this time. So you can see that despite good results, the risk considerations are somewhat complex.

What I mean is that the company's upsides - meaning Gemini's hub-and-spoke approach for better asset utilization, coupled with increased investments in logistics for better efficiency and capturing market share, and a higher percentage of returns from longer-term contracts (which are all advantages that the company is having), will not meaningfully make Maersk (in my view) better than the current valuation levels.

And that, dear readers, is the reason why I don't raise my fair value or my targets as of 3Q25, despite the quarter being a good one.

Let me unpack this targeting and fair value logic for you.

Unpacking my Maersk Valuation targets - upside is limited

In my last article, I gave the company a PT of 11,800 DKK/share. I'm raising this target to 12,200/share as of this time. Why? Because the company has increased its EBITDA for 2025-2026E, and I believe this estimate to be valid and realistic. So, based on a 3x EV/EBITDA multiple, it would imply my forecasted $8.55B EBITDA, compared to below $8.3B previously. I cannot get away from the Ocean segment, which still contributes over 50% of the company's revenue. It continues to be the driver of targets. My longer-term targets for Maersk, which also come into play in my fair value of about 15,500 DKK, is a 2-3% revenue growth over time, slightly above average, and a good growth rate for the Terminals business. I don't expect revolutions here, but Gemini will make sure that assets are being utilized efficiently here.

Dividend safety for Maersk is also solid, as the company will continue to pay one. Maersk's dividend is based on its earnings, which have a tendency to fluctuate, but even forecasts for the dividend have it staying above 60-70/share over the next 5 years. You should also look at the company's dividend stability outside of an upswing or downturn, such as the 2016-2020 periods.

So the company does pay a well-covered dividend, insofar that's it's "a dividend", though if you're looking for dividend stability and a guaranteed (or close to) yield, you may look at some of the alternatives I present, which are safer. However, over time (a 10-year period), you can see that the company always pays a dividend, and that dividend is well-covered by earnings.

The main driver against putting this company higher than that 3x EV/EBITDA is the uncertainty, I believe, to is endemic to Maersk. The company can be as efficient as it wants, as good as it wants, but it cannot affect global freight and logistics trends. These are subject to complex geopolitical macro, to tariffs, to competition in shipping prices (in fact, even environmental risks can come up, with the examples in Panama not that long ago being the latest one). All of these trends and impacts are very visible for anyone looking at the company - just look at the period between 2021 to 2022 to 2023. Now things are, compared to 2023, on their way up again. While these ups and downs are not forecastable specifically, the fact that there will be ups and downs is, in my view, forecastable with a very high conviction.

Because of that, I try to work with what I consider to be "extreme patience" in investing here. While I believe Maersk to be attractive at the right price, that price is not here. There are logistics companies with significantly better and higher upside (albeit at a high uncertainty as well). The entire logistics space is volatile, more so than perhaps at any one time in decades.

That doesn't mean we should count out some of the more legacy companies, however.

The primary alternative to Maersk, which I consider to be a "HOLD" here, is UPS. UPS might not have been interesting above $120/share, but I also didn't buy it at $120/share. I started buying sub-$100/share, and doubled my position to over 1.7% in August at $88/share. I'm now in the green, and the company is at $97/share roughly. There's still a 6.7% yield, and even to a conservative forecast, a good upside that doesn't even require much growth from the company here. I forecast at 15.8x, which is the lowest multiple compared to the 18x+ 20-year P/E average, and I still get a 22% upside annualized from this investment.

This is what value investing is about to me. finding those A-rated global companies, with a massive yield, where the market is telling me that a certain company is "bad".

If I disagree, as I do here, I will go firmly against the grain.

But Maersk is a "HOLD" here, and the risks are as follows.

Risks to my thesis

The risks to my thesis can still be viewed in two ways. Either if the company does significantly better than expected, my rating results in missing out on further growth and profit (though again, I do have a position in the company and would see part of this profit).

Then there is the risk of a worse performance than expected if things go south. As mentioned, in the scenario of a significant recession in the U.S., I would cut my target below 10,500 (unlikely now) at least to reflect what I believe to be meaningful volume and profit declines, even if the future seems to mean fewer volumes from the U.S. in general.

So it's a very fluid situation, which is why I would argue at this valuation and at this macro, Maersk shouldn't necessarily be a focus for you. If we see a price below 11,000, that may change, but for now, my PT update is to 12,200 DKK/share, and my rating is hold. For AMKBF, the ADR, this comes to a new PT of ~$1,900, which reiterates and confirms my Hold rating given the current price of over $1,900.

My thesis is as follows.

Thesis

  • This is one of the world's most important shipping companies. At a good valuation, this company is, to me, a no-nonsense "Buy" with no looking back. The flow of goods dictates the state of our national economies, which makes the company a major player in macroeconomics and trade.
  • I view the company currently as fairly valued, with a long-term upside but a shorter-term downside due to exposure to the logistics and freight cycle.
  • It's a "HOLD" here to me, and I upgraded the target to 12,200 DKK/share, for 2025. However, as mentioned above, there are better alternatives in the logistics space anyway, so the "HOLD" rating isn't that big of an issue, in waiting for a better entry/add price.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them:

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative, and well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a valuation target of 12,200 DKK/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "HOLD" rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Simon Property Group: Appears Fairly Valued, But Dividend Growth Could Ignite The Next Rally (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

Simon Property Group (SPG) is a REIT I've been bullish on the past few years. This was mainly due to the stock being undervalued at the time. Since my last coverage, SPG has outpaced the S&P (SP500).

As a result, I think significant upside is unlikely, as I think the REIT is now trading near fair value. They've increased their dividend, a sign of growing cash flows and strong fundamentals.

Moreover, SPG could see tailwinds from lower base rates, leading to more upside over the next 12 to 24 months.

In this article, I discuss Simon Property Group's latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

Previous Buy Case

I last covered Simon Property Group back in August in an article titled: Guidance Raise, Dividend Hike, And 5% Yield - What's Not To Like?

As previously mentioned, the stock has beaten the S&P, up 10.40% compared to 7.51% for the index. During Q2 earnings, SPG managed to beat analysts estimates on both their top and bottom lines. This allowed management to raise guidance.

They also raised the dividend 4.9% to $2.15, placing it above the pre-pandemic level after cutting it in 2020. Although tariffs presented risks that potentially limited upside, I remained bullish due to their fundamentals, anticipated rate cuts, and upside to their price target of $182.

Strong Quarter

Simon Property Group reported their Q3 earnings in early November and reported another strong quarter. FFO of $3.22 beat by $0.09 and rose 5.6% from $3.05 the prior year.

Revenue of $1.6 billion grew 8.1% and beat by $190 million. SPG's strong performance was driven by an 8% increase in lease income, higher occupancy, and net operating income. Back-to-school traffic was a main contributing factor.

Occupancy stood at 96.4% at malls & outlets and 99.4% for the Mills. During the quarter, they signed over 1,000 leases totaling 4 million square feet. The prior year's quarter saw 1,200 leases signed, also totaling 4 million square feet.

While leases signed were relatively flat, SPG saw higher numbers pretty much across the board. Occupancy at malls increased from 96.2%, while the Mills saw an 80 basis point increase.

Lease income was significantly lower at 3% last year compared to 8% for the quarter. Domestic NOI saw a slight downtick during the quarter from 5.4%, while portfolio NOI slightly increased from 5%.

During the quarter, both were 5.1% and 5.2%, respectively. This performance allowed management to raise its FFO outlook for 2025. FFO is now expected in a range of $12.60 - $12.70, up from $12.45 - $12.65.

Future Growth

While Simon Property Group displayed strong financials and fundamentals, the company is positioned well to see future growth. The REIT acquired the remaining 12% interest in Taubman Realty Group. The acquisition is expected to be accretive and drive NOI & FFO growth.

Growth is expected to be recognized in 2026, and benefits fully realized by 2027. In Q3, the REIT also started construction on multiple projects, totaling $1.25 billion at a blended yield of 9%. So, SPG looks poised to see strong growth going forward. And this will likely lead to upside over the long term.

Another Dividend Hike

During my last article, I touched on the REIT's 4.9% dividend raise to $2.15 that put the dividend above their pre-pandemic level. In early November, SPG again raised its dividend, this time another $0.05 to $2.20 a share.

Using their dividend estimate of $8.53, this gives them a well-covered dividend with a payout ratio of 67.4% using their guidance midpoint. This gives the REIT ample retained capital for organic growth and room for further increases down the line.

Balance Sheet

Simon Property Group's balance sheet was in tip-top shape with an A credit rating. Despite having over $1.6 billion in debt maturing in 2026, liquidity was strong with $9.5 billion. Their debt had a weighted-average interest rate of 3.98% with 6.9 years to maturity.

Appears Fairly Valued

At $182 a share at the time of writing, Simon Property Group appears to be fairly valued here, having caught up to the price target Wall Street assigned during my last thesis.

While I do think more upside is possible over the next year or two if we see more macro certainty, I do think significant upside is limited.

According to FAST Graphs, SPG's price seems to have finally caught up to their fundamentals. Over the next two years, they offer little upside to their price target of $191 a share.

Risks

During the quarter, SPG displayed strong financials, driven by increases in traffic and seasonality. While the consumer remains resilient for now, 2026 could be a different story if jobs data continues to weaken.

If so, I expect the REIT to see slowdowns in traffic, potentially leading to lower NOI & FFO growth. The economy seems to be showing cracks despite resilient spending, which could be propped up by seasonality with Black Friday and the Christmas holiday around the corner. But a recession in the next year or two is likely if we experience further weakening.

Conclusion

Simon Property Group remains a premier REIT, and their latest quarter displayed this. The recent dividend hike is also a testament to their fundamentals and outlook going forward.

Well-covered by cash flows with a strong balance sheet, I expect single, mid-digit dividend hikes for the foreseeable future. And these could propel more upside.

However, this may be limited as they seem fairly valued here. Although rate cuts could provide tailwinds, Simon Property Group's valuation and the potential to see further economic weakening are the main reasons I'm downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

data.title: Oracle: Irrational Post-Earnings Sell-Off As AI Growth Pipeline Remains Intact
data.description:
In my last coverage on Oracle Corporation (ORCL), I rated the stock a cautious buy as the market digested the $300B OpenAI deal. Since then, credit spreads have widened on fears around Oracle's debt strategy and the uncertainty around how OpenAI will fund the $300B deal.

Given the double-digit selloff post Q2 FY26 earnings, I considered revising my bull case to see if it is worth readjusting my rating. Overall, despite the top-line miss, I don't see a major deterioration in the growth story (which starts in mid-2027, once the OpenAI deployment begins). The revenue guidance for FY26 was unchanged, and the company obliterated EPS estimates.

On the negative side, the capital intensity of the AI buildout, with negative FCF, a sharply higher multi-year CapEx trajectory, and rising debt, is pushing CDS wider, with some estimates forecasting a spread of up to 200 bps, the highest since the 2008 financial crisis.

Overall, I continue to see upside in the OCI segment and the AI CapEx cycle but believe returns will be back-end loaded to 2027. In the near term, I expect shares to consolidate around the $200 support, although I don't expect a V-shaped recovery in the next 3-6 months.

Q2 FY26 Earnings Overview

There are three items that raised my eyebrow in the earnings results. First, the company reported consolidated revenue of $16.06B (up 14% yoy) vs. the Street's estimate of $6.19B. Even though the company obliterated EPS expectations on the bottom line ($2.26 reported vs. the Street's $1.64), investors wanted a clean beat on the top line as well.

In my view, the beat on EPS was not a surprising event for investors, given the $2.7B pre-tax tailwind on the sale of the Ampere stake

The second item is the revenue of the cloud segment. This item came in below the Street's expectations, at $8B vs. the Street's estimate of $8.04. Within this broader segment, OCI reported revenue of $4.1B (up 66% yoy), with GPU-related revenue up 177% yoy.

Cloud database services grew 30%, with autonomous database revenue up 43% and multicloud consumption up a surprising 817% yoy. I considered including below Clay Magouyrk's remarks during the last earnings call, given the upside potential of “billions in identified pipeline”:

We launched 11 Multicloud regions this quarter bringing us to 45 regions live across AWS, Azure and GCP with 27 more planned over the next month. We see increasing customer demand with billions in identified pipeline. We launched 2 important programs this quarter for Multicloud. The first is Multicloud Universal Credits, which enables customers to commit once to Oracle database services and use them anywhere in any cloud with the same price and flexibility. The second is our Multicloud channel reseller program, which enables customers to procure Oracle database services through their preferred channel partners.

The third item that raised my eyebrow was the negative free cash flow of $10B. The main driver was a CapEx of $12B due to heavy investment to support cloud and infrastructure growth. From a timing perspective, this spike in CapEx makes sense given the recent general availability of Nvidia's flagship GB300 AI accelerator:

Rather the equipment CapEx is purchased very late in the data center production cycle, allowing us to quickly convert cash spent into revenues earned as we provision cloud services to our contracted and committed customers.

To me, the spike in CapEx makes complete sense, as hyperscalers want to isolate themselves from the obsolescence of older GPU models. This strategy is capital-intensive, given that some sources quote the GB300 NVL72 rack system at about USD $3 million per rack vs. $2.6M for GB200 NVL72.

From a guidance perspective, management now expects fiscal 2026 CapEx to be about $15B higher than the previous estimate, totaling $50B. To me, this is a positive sign heading into calendar 2026, as it shows that demand for computing is far from reaching a plateau. As a side note, I believe the AI trade will last as long as the main hyperscalers grow their CapEx spending. As soon as CapEx cools off, the tech sector (particularly hyperscalers and neocloud players) could see a return to reality. I project we are far from this point, although I don't dare to project a date given that I don't see any weakness in demand from the top 4 hyperscalers and now Oracle.

Moving on to RPO, the company added $68B sequentially, totaling $523.3B (up 433% yoy). From a conversion perspective, RPO expected to be recognized in the next 12 months grew at 40% yoy (vs. 25% in Q1 and 21% a year ago). However, I wasn't particularly excited about the fact that FY’26 total revenue guidance remains unchanged at $67 billion:

The result is we now expect $4 billion of additional revenue in FY '27. Our full year FY '26 revenue expectation of $67 billion remains unchanged. However, given the added RPO this quarter that can be monetized quickly starting next year.

So, the recently added RPO will contribute to only $4 billion of additional revenue in FY ’27. So, I don't quite agree with Douglas Kehring's comments that RPO added this quarter that can be “monetized quickly.” As seen below, the Street only expects a meaningful acceleration in growth starting in fiscal 2028 (mid-calendar 2027).

Naturally, the meat of the bone in terms of growth is the $300B contract with OpenAI announced on September 10, 2025. This is a 5-year contract, with multiple sources suggesting that it begins in 2027 (coincidentally, when the Street expects top-line growth to accelerate). Until then, revenue growth is decent (between low and high 20%) but not spectacular. That's why the optimism after the OpenAI deal faded so quickly.

Risks

Oracle’s CDS has recently been moving sharply higher because credit markets are repricing the risk of its very large, debt-funded AI and data center expansion.

More recently, Morgan Stanley suggested that the spread could approach 200 bps, the highest since the 2008 financial crisis. This is naturally pressuring the sentiment around this stock.

If we compare Oracle to the rest of the Mag 7 hyperscalers, its debt-to-equity ratio is significantly higher, sitting at over 460%.

Back in September, the company issued about $18bn of bonds, with Barclays stating that its credit rating could fall to BBB-, very close to junk territory, if execution or cash generation disappoints. In my view, the skepticism seems to be around how OpenAI is going to fund the $300B deployment, given its limited upside. Some sources estimate OpenAI's revenue in the low- to mid-teens billions of dollars in 2025 and around $100B revenue by 2027. Therefore, funding this deployment with revenue alone may be challenging (at best). In my view, OpenAI is most likely in need of debt.

Valuation

Despite the selloff post Q1 FY26 results, the stock is trading at 45x next year's earnings. When compared to the Mag 7 hyperscalers, this multiple is the richest in the group.

Overall, I don't see an undervalued company here. A bet on Oracle is a bet on growth starting in 2027. Until then, the stock is at the mercy of short-term traders, who are naturally influenced by the fears around debt and OpenAI's liquidity to fund the $300B deployment.

Conclusion

After Q2 FY26 earnings, shares are down double digits, retesting the $200 support level.

I have to say that the chart above is not particularly encouraging. Shares are cheaper now than pre-OpenAI announcement, and sentiment hasn't improved after the company missed top-line estimates in Q2.

Overall, I am cautiously bullish over a longer-term horizon. Until mid-2027, growth is expected in the low to high 20s percent. The spike in revenue only begins in mid-2027. Until then, the pessimism around the high debt and the mechanisms of how OpenAI will fund the deal could pressure the stock. I expect shares to consolidate around the $200s, although I don’t think we’ll see a breakout in the very near term.

data.title: EM Bonds Outshine DM As Fundamentals Drive Stability
data.description:
EM bonds have remained resilient and outperformed DM bonds in 2025 as strong fundamentals and positioning drive continued defensive behavior.

The VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF (EMBX) was up 0.79% in November, compared to 0.83% for its benchmark. Year to date, EMBX is up 17.38%, compared to 15.47% for its benchmark, the 50% J.P. Morgan Government Bond Index - Emerging Markets Global Diversified (GBI-EM) and 50% J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI) and 8.09% and 8.95% for the Global Agg and 10-Year Treasuries, respectively. In the past five years, EMBX has returned 4.3% per year, compared to 1.76% for its benchmark and negative -2.29% and negative -2.31% per year for the Global Agg and 10-Year Treasuries, respectively. South Africa continued to outperform, Chile benefited from market-friendly elections, while small exposures in “frontier” Zambia local currency and Bolivia hard currency rounded out these winners. Our underweight in Poland local led underperformers, and Uganda local also generated some underperformance. We pulled in our horns even more in November, after very strong performance. Local currency exposure is now even lower at 40%. We have no meaningful overweights in majors outside of Chile in local currency, and don’t own Indian local currency. Carry is 6.4%, yield to worst (YTW) is 7.4%, and duration is 5.5, right near benchmark duration.

The NAV is not necessarily the same as the ETF 's intraday trading value. Investors should not expect to buy or sell shares at NAV.

Notwithstanding the foregoing, the Adviser has agreed to pay the offering costs until at least May 1, 2027. “Other Expenses” have been restated to reflect current fees.

Emerging markets keep running the table. Despite a spike in global equity volatility associated with the AI/tech narrative challenge, EM bonds keep chugging along like defensive assets. The Kospi was an epicenter of the volatility spike, yet the Korean won (KRW) and Korean govvies rallied, and for good reasons – Korea is an EM “graduate”, like many others, particularly in Asia. EMBX has exposure to Korean government bonds and given the spike weaker in the Japanese yen (JPY) and Japanese government bonds (JGBs), we thought it might be worth a brief mention. Perhaps it’s an indulgence on the part of your PM, who has ingrained in him great worry (always good in bonds) over any developments in funding markets, particularly Japan’s. Japan was and remains an important source of “carry trade” funding. And this is always the moment where we remind investors that Japan is a poster child for our thesis of “fiscal dominance” in the DM – it makes sense for Japanese investors faced with rising interest rates and a weakening currency to seek safer harbors offshore, whether it is US tech stocks or South African government bonds. Anyhoo, when funding currencies flip, a balance sheet stock becomes a flow very quickly, thus the worry. But, Korea is one of our favorite “graduates”, characterized by net creditor status, in US dollar (USD) terms, strong positive net international investment position (NIIP), good fiscal policy, and a central bank focused on inflation. It, too, therefore, owns and funds a lot of offshore assets, but is not subject to fiscal dominance. So, how did it fare in this vol spike that your indulgent PM worried about? It fared well, due to large current account surpluses, and this despite penned-up USD selling on the part of exporters. Equally importantly the bias towards offshore assets on the part of onshore savers is a structural feature and one that is now well-established and managed. The demand for offshore assets is supported by policymakers accommodating savers in a demographic decline (you kind-of have to own companies offshore in that situation). The National Pension System (NPS) actively manages hedging policies on FX (there’s a tactical and a strategic hedge bucket, but we won’t get into that here). The bottom line is that because NPS’ foreign assets are now on the order of the country’s FX reserves, even a 10% hedge implies the capacity to supply roughly USD 30–50 billion to the FX market via derivatives or asset sales when activated, which is large relative to average daily USD/KRW turnover. You see the results of these initial conditions in the exhibit below. KRW vol spiked lower recently, due to NPS action.

Exhibit 1 – KRW Vol at Lows Despite Kospi Vol at Highs

Source: Bloomberg, as of December 3, 2025.

Is it JPY, JGB, or JOL (Japan out of luck)? Japan is the poster child for DM fiscal dominance and has an important stock of offshore assets we care about in its “carry trade”. The key meaning is that Japan is problematic, period; there’s no evidence of contagion, if anything many Asian bond markets are behaving like flight-to-quality assets, as they should. It’s only an overly worried or indulgent PM invoking risks to a “graduate” EM like Korea, when both rates and the exchange rate are weakening in Japan. And the exhibit below shows that Japan is behaving differently, however subtly, relative to Korea. In the chart below you will see that JPY vol has not had the secular decline that characterized KRW vol. Moreover, JPY vol rose a bit recently on just a very minor uptick in Nikkei vol. Thanks for your indulgence. Fiscal dominance characterizes DMs, not EMs, and the recent bout of global risk off supports this once again.

Exhibit 2 – JPY Volatility Remained Low Despite Spikes in the Nikkei

Source: Bloomberg, as of December 3, 2025.

Exposure Types and Significant Changes

The changes to our top positions are summarized below. Our largest positions in November were Brazil, Malaysia, South Africa, Thailand, and Chile:

  • We increased our local currency exposure in the Czech Republic. Czech local bonds continue to look attractive vs. fundamentals and might benefit from the peak U.S. Dollar bullishness. In addition, the new government is not making any suspicious moves on the fiscal front. In terms of our investment process, this improved the policy and technical test scores for the country.
  • We also increased our hard currency sovereign exposure in India, Malaysia, the Philippines, Peru, Morocco, Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, and Poland. The move reflected our intention to piggyback on global duration (which strengthened the technical test scores for the countries in question), but in some cases (Poland, Malaysia) the shift also reflected our year-end de-risking from local bonds (which posted significant year-to-date total returns).
  • Finally, we increased our hard currency sovereign exposure in Laos and Bolivia. The bond in Laos was an attractively priced new issue, whereas Bolivian sovereign bonds continue to benefit from the market-friendly results of the presidential election, which improved the policy/politics test score for the country.
  • We reduced our local currency exposure in Mexico, Brazil, Turkey, Uganda, and South Africa. The year-end de-risking (due to tighter liquidity) is a key reason here, especially in less liquid names like Uganda, where the impact of global moves can be amplified. Additional country-specific factors included (1) the worsening political test score in Mexico on the back of mass protests against the deteriorating domestic security situation; (2) less attractive valuations in Brazil; and (3) a limited room for rate cuts in Turkey due to rising inflation expectations (which worsened the policy test score).
  • We also reduced our hard currency sovereign exposure in Argentina, Cote d’Ivoire, and Ecuador. We took profits in Argentina after the midterms rally, as there are unresolved issues related to the accumulation of international reserves and the exchange rate mechanism, which worsen the policy test score for the country. Cote d’Ivoire’s very tight spreads and less attractive valuations made it vulnerable to spillovers from a potential debt restructuring situation in Senegal, worsening the technical test score for the country. The referendum’s failure in Ecuador raised doubts about President Noboa’s ability to advance reforms, worsening the policy/politics test score for the country.
  • Finally, we reduced our hard currency corporate exposure in Singapore and Hong Kong. Our focus in Singapore was on a high-yield corporate bond (with some ties to real estate in the region), which can get affected by the year-end’s tight liquidity. Regarding Hong Kong, we decided to take profits due the company’s exchange of outstanding perpetual securities.
