data.title: The Fed's 'Gift' Is A Trap
data.description:
The Federal Reserve just cut interest rates at its December meeting, albeit amidst a sharply divided Federal Reserve in the midst of one of the most overvalued stock markets (SPY) in history. This leaves broad indexes vulnerable to downside risk, while the reduced Fed refund rate favors stagnation-resistant infrastructure and bullion leases, leaving them as superior risk-adjusted opportunities. This is because the Fed is cautiously easing interest rates while also beginning to buy bonds (BND) once again, even as its own projections see that inflation is likely going to remain above its target 2% rate for years to come and that the labor market is only weakening slightly.

At the same time, while their easing of interest rates and pivot away from quantitative tightening mean that they are giving an upward lift to inflation, at the same time, expectations that the Fed's three consecutive rate cuts are going to provide lasting legs to the bull market may also be mistaken. This is particularly because the dot plot still implies higher-for-longer interest rates relative to recent policy after the Great Financial Crisis, at the same time as major market valuation models indicate that the market remains heavily overvalued. This means that the higher-for-longer interest rates are a weight on valuation multiples that are already quite elevated, increasing strain on the market and potentially leading to a correction.

In addition, upcoming CPI and PCE data releases, monthly payroll data, treasury refunding announcements, and ongoing Fed commentary, as well as a soon-to-be-released replacement for Jerome Powell at the Fed, mean that there are a lot of potential catalysts that could continue to prop up the market in the near term, or potentially send it tumbling lower. However, in my view, the risk-reward remains quite heavy to the downside in the broader market indexes. In today's article, I am going to dig into why in more detail.

The December FOMC Meeting: A Deeper Look

While the Federal Reserve cut its federal funds target rate by 25 basis points to 3.5% to 3.75% at the December FOMC meeting, which was the third straight cut and the lowest rate in roughly three years, there is a lot more to the story than simply the headline interest rate cut.

First of all, the economic outlook moving forward based on the Summary of Economic Projections is better than previously thought, with 2026 real GDP growth increased to 2.3%. Unemployment is expected to remain in the mid-4% range, while core inflation is expected to continue to remain higher than the target 2% rate for the next several years, and not reach it until the end of 2028.

Therefore, with growth expected to be a bit higher and inflation expected to drift lower only very slowly, the dot plot currently signals just one rate cut expected in 2026, though there is a wide difference of opinion among the FOMC committee, which indicates that things could swing in differing directions based on incoming data.

Market Valuation: Dangerously Overvalued

Overall, the market cheered the release of information from the Fed, with the Dow (DIA) up significantly today, and the S&P 500 (VOO) nearing record highs, while the Nasdaq (QQQ) also continued its strong bullish run. This was largely attributed to the fact that the Fed seemed more bullish on the economy than previously expected, which gave investors increased hope that the Fed has more wiggle room to be able to navigate supporting its dual mandate of fighting inflation and supporting full employment.

Meanwhile, the market appears to only have limited further upside from a fundamental perspective, though numerous valuation metrics, whether it be the Buffett indicator or the Shiller CAPE, and several others, indicate that the market is significantly overvalued right now. In fact, famous investors like Howard Marks and Ray Dalio believe that the market is likely in a bubble and that total returns on an annualized basis over the next decade could well hover around 0%. In fact, they believe that a lot of forward returns have been pulled forward due to suppressed real interest rates over the past decade plus since the Great Financial Crisis, as well as significant liquidity from quantitative easing and significant bullish expectations about the future of AI that still have a ways to go to fully deliver on the bullish projections being priced into stocks right now.

As such, in my view, the U.S. equity indexes continue to face asymmetric downside risk, as a very bullish scenario will have to play out for the economy and AI for the current valuations to be justified. Moreover, there are so many risks to the downside, from a fragile labor market to persistently high inflation to huge CapEx bets being made on AI that may or may not play out. And on top of that, you have considerable geopolitical risk with tensions boiling over between China and Japan and war continuing to rage in Eastern Europe.

Growing Division and Structural Interest Rates

Additionally, moving forward, it will be interesting to see if the Fed will be able to respond strongly in one direction or the other due to the growing division on the board, as three FOMC members dissented against the 25 basis point December rate cut, which is the largest dissent on the FOMC since 2019, though one of those three dissenters favored a larger 50 basis point reduction, while two stood for no change to the interest rate. The dot plot clusters seem to suggest a floor for the target federal funds rate in the low to mid threes through 2028, which is far above the pre-COVID norm, thereby signaling that they expect structurally higher interest rates and therefore inflation even after the cycle concludes.

This is especially noteworthy given that AI is expected to be significantly deflationary, though the benefits of AI may not kick in until later in the decade than 2028 in a manner that makes a material difference in the inflation number. This is particularly true given that the CapEx required to build out the AI industry is inflationary in the near term due to the strong demand and consumption of capital for it.

Ultimately, though, if there is a higher real interest rate, it is going to put more pressure on equities as they have soared higher in the years since the COVID crash and now stand far above where they were before COVID, despite interest rates also appearing likely to remain materially higher for longer. That means that unless significant growth materializes from AI, the stock market is likely going to have a major day of reckoning.

Investor Takeaway

So what does this mean for the market? Well, it means that while the rate cut, coupled with more bullish economic news, understandably boosted the market's mood in the near term and, in our view, does bode well for things, at least in the near term, we continue to think that the long-term upside potential for equity indexes remains very limited. This is because there remains significant downside risk if either the AI boom does not play out or macro risks like budget deficits, persistent inflation, and or geopolitical risks manifest themselves in a more meaningful manner, which could cause the stock market to pull back very sharply.

Therefore, I am steering clear of major indexes and instead am favoring stagflation-resistant, undervalued, high-yielding opportunities, especially in the midstream space (AMLP), of which I detailed some of my top picks recently here. Additionally, I like gold bullion leases because they are fairly attractive stagflation plays, as they are pretty defensive and inflation-resistant investments that also deliver an attractive yield that makes them superior total return opportunities to simply investing in gold (GLD) and silver (SLV) ETFs. As a result, stagflation-resistant, high-yield, undervalued, investment-grade infrastructure companies and gold bullion leases are my two largest investments in my 7% yielding Core, International, and Retirement portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: VICI Properties: Doubling Down On This 6%+ Yield Right Now
data.description:
My audience is overwhelmingly focused on dividend growth stocks. Given my pseudonym of Kody's Dividends, that's hardly a surprise.

As such, it's also probably not a stretch to assert that most are familiar with the term "yield trap." This is used to characterize a stock with an exceptionally high dividend yield. Oftentimes, this is a high single-digit percentage yield for C-Corps or a double-digit percentage yield for REITs/midstream companies.

Avoiding yield traps is arguably one of the most important aspects of being a dividend growth investor. That brings to mind the following quote of famed financial writer and market strategist Raymond DeVoe Jr.:

More money has been lost reaching for yield than at the point of a gun.

This is backed up by the hard data, too. Data from Hartford Funds shows that dividend cutters and eliminators have returned -0.89% annually from 1973 through 2024. This isn't even taking into account the loads of cumulative inflation that have materialized over the past five decades and counting.

Today, I'm going to be revisiting VICI Properties (VICI). Despite its elevated dividend yield, it's anything but a yield trap in my opinion. When I last covered VICI with a buy rating in January, I thought the company could keep growing AFFO per share at a steady clip. The REIT's net leverage ratio also seemed to be viable. Finally, shares looked to be moderately undervalued.

Eleven months later, I'm reaffirming my Buy rating. VICI's growth profile looks to be holding up. The company's balance sheet remains investment-grade. VICI's shares have also become a more enticing value.

VICI Properties Is Humming Along

On October 30th, VICI released its financial results for the third quarter ended September 30th, 2025. The company's total revenue grew by 4.4% year-over-year to $1.01 billion during the quarter. For more perspective, this topped Seeking Alpha's analyst consensus in the quarter by $5.1 million. VICI's share count only ticked up by 1.9% over the year-ago period, which implies modest per-share revenue growth as well.

What was behind the company's top-line growth for the third quarter? The majority of VICI's total revenue growth came from a surge in income from loans and securities. This revenue source jumped 68.2% year-over-year to $61 million during the quarter. That was fueled by the origination and funding of debt investments and the interest income stemming from higher principal balances outstanding.

The other notable variable behind VICI's revenue growth in the third quarter came from higher leasing revenue. Leasing revenue increased by 1.9% over the year-ago period to $918.8 million for the quarter. Capital investments into the Venetian Resort and annual rent escalators from its other lease agreements were responsible for the growth in leasing revenue during the quarter.

VICI's AFFO per share rose by 5.3% year-over-year to $0.60 in the third quarter. Disciplined cost management helped its AFFO margin expand by about 170 basis points to 63.3% for the quarter. That's how AFFO per share growth surpassed revenue growth during the quarter.

Moving forward, VICI has ways that it can drive more AFFO per share growth for shareholders. For one, the company's minimum, contractual annual rent escalations provide 1.7% same-store rent growth. On its own, that's already almost 6x the 0.3% rate of select triple net lease REITs.

VICI has room for more rent growth upside, too. In 2025, 42% of VICI's rent roll was tied to CPI-linked escalators. Over the long term, this is set to gradually rise to around 90% by 2035.

Its modest payout ratio allows it to retain AFFO for reinvestment in other projects as well. That's another growth driver for the REIT.

What's more, VICI recently announced a $1.16 billion acquisition of seven Nevada-based casino properties from Golden Entertainment in a sale-leaseback deal. This will add $87 million of initial annual cash rent (with an initial lease term of 30 years and four five-year renewal options), which works out to a 7.5% cap rate.

Beginning in the third year of the lease, annual rent escalation will be 2%. As of Q2 2025, the property-level rent coverage ratio of 1.9x points to strong unit economics. VICI is also included in a right of first offer on future developments like Colorado Belle, which could position the partnership for growth down the line. The deal is expected to close in mid-2026.

For these reasons, the FAST Graphs analyst consensus is for AFFO per share to grow by 3.4% in 2026 to $2.45 (following a 4.9% consensus growth rate to $2.37 in 2025). For 2027, another 2.9% increase in AFFO per share to $2.52 is being projected.

VICI is also financially sound. As of September 30th, 2025, the company's net leverage ratio was 5.0x. This is on the low end of its targeted range of between 5.0x and 5.5x. The company's debt profile is attractive as well, with 99% of its debt at fixed rates and 83% unsecured. Maturities in the coming years are manageable, ranging from as little as $750 million in unsecured bonds in 2031 to as much as $2 billion in unsecured bonds in 2028 and 2030, respectively. Its $3 billion revolving credit facility isn't set to expire until 2032. As a result, VICI possesses a BBB- S&P credit rating with a stable outlook (unless otherwise sourced or hyperlinked, all info in this subhead was according to VICI's Q3 2025 Earnings Press Release, VICI's Q3 2025 10-Q Filing, and VICI's November 2025 Investor Presentation).

Fair Value Is Now Around $35 A Share

In the last 11 months, VICI's shares have been essentially flat, posting a -1% total return in that time. Concerns over Las Vegas visitation and the underperformance of REITs in general were contributing factors to these stagnant returns.

The big picture for me is unchanged, however. VICI's shares offer a compelling risk-reward dynamic in my view. The forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 11.4 is a downright bargain. That's an entire turn cheaper than the previous forward 12-month P/AFFO ratio of 12.5 when I last covered the stock.

VICI's current valuation remains well below the seven-year average P/AFFO ratio of 15.7, according to FAST Graphs. That was down from the previous value of 16.2 because data from the lower valuation year of 2024 has since been included in the data set.

I believe that VICI can command a fair value P/AFFO ratio of 14.1. This would be one standard deviation less than the seven-year average P/AFFO ratio.

My rationale is that VICI's growth prospects are arguably holding up. I'm confident that the REIT can consistently grow AFFO per share by 3% to 4% annually.

The headwind to VICI's valuation remains the forward-looking interest rate atmosphere. The 10-year U.S. Treasury yield (US10Y) is likely to remain elevated in the high-3% to low-4% range for the foreseeable future. For context, that would be materially higher than the seven-year average of 2.6%, per Macrotrends. This is how I arrive at my fair value P/AFFO ratio of 14.1.

As of today, the calendar year 2025 is 94% complete. That leaves another 6% of 2025 and 94% of 2026 yet to come over the next 12 months. This is how I arrive at a forward 12-month AFFO per share input of $2.45.

Using my fair value P/AFFO ratio of 14.1, I get a fair value of $35 a share. Compared to the current $28 share price (as of December 10th, 2025), that equates to a 19% discount to fair value.

If VICI returns to fair value and lives up to growth expectations, it could have a 38% upside ahead through 2026. By the end of 2030, the REIT could return 12% annually.

A 6%+ Payout With Room To Grow

VICI's 6.5% forward dividend yield registers comfortably above the real estate sector's 4.9% median forward yield. The REIT's slight selloff in 2025, coupled with its recent dividend hike (more on that in a moment), has widened the forward yield spread by 20 basis points since I last covered it. This is enough for Seeking Alpha's Quant System to award B+ and A- grades for forward dividend yield and overall dividend yield, respectively (the latter of which was up from the previous B+ grade).

VICI's immense immediate income is also backed up by its AFFO generation. The company is poised to record an AFFO payout ratio in the mid-70% range for 2025. That's right around the 75% payout ratio that it targets, and, according to the Zen Research Terminal, is superior to the 90% payout ratio that rating agencies like to see from the industry.

This is why the Zen Research Terminal anticipates a 1.3% risk of a dividend reduction in the next 12 months. In the next average recession, the probability of a payout cut rises to 4.5%. That's what accounts for the B+ grade from the Quant System for overall dividend safety (up from the prior B grade).

Not surprisingly, I believe that VICI is set up to extend its seven-year dividend growth streak. As it is, this is more established than the sector median of 1.4 years. This explains the Quant System's B grade for overall dividend consistency (down from the previous A- grade).

Following a 4% raise in its quarterly dividend per share to $0.45 in September, the Quant System thinks similar dividend growth is likely from VICI. The Quant System forecasts 4.3% annual forward dividend per share growth from the REIT. That would be over 200 basis points better than the sector median forecast of 2.2%. This warrants a B grade from the Quant System for overall dividend growth (down from the previous A grade).

Risks To Consider

VICI is a qualitative REIT. However, the company still has risks that could meaningfully change the investment thesis.

One key concern is Las Vegas visitor volume thus far in 2025. The latest data shows that the city's visitor volume through the first 10 months of the year was 32.3 million people. That's down 7.6% or 2.6 million people from 2024.

Historically, Las Vegas has experienced downturns in tourism in tandem with economic downturns or recessions. At face value, I think that's worrisome that visitor volume is down in a non-recessionary environment. However, I tend to agree with the take from local economist Mike PeQueen.

Data suggests that Las Vegas is emerging into a higher-end market, with it becoming the destination for sports entertainment as of late. This is evidenced by the Super Bowl, the Grand Prix, and WWE WrestleMania coming to town in recent years. As a result, the latest data from the Las Vegas Convention and Visitors Authority Research Center shows Clark County gross gaming revenue to be up 1%.

From this perspective, it looks like Las Vegas will be fine. Of course, it's worth watching gaming revenue for Clark County moving forward and visitor numbers. That's because about 48% of VICI's total rent revenue is derived from Las Vegas.

Aside from geographic concentration, the other key risk to the REIT is its tenant concentration. As of September 30th, 2025, Caesars Entertainment and MGM Resorts made up 74% of its total annualized cash rent (39% and 35%, respectively). If these tenants encounter any financial issues, VICI could have difficulty collecting rent for the first time since its inception in a worst-case scenario. That could pressure cash flows, resulting in a dividend cut.

Summary: I'm Adding More To This Core Holding

VICI has catalysts to keep growing for years to come. There's the organic growth that's made possible by annual lease escalators. Then, there's the fact that VICI retains plenty of AFFO for selective investments and accompanying business growth. The company's balance sheet is stable as well.

These solid fundamentals also look to come at a dirt-cheap valuation. Shares are deeply undervalued, with nearly 40% upside potential by the end of 2026 and strong return potential through 2030. Earlier this month, I marginally added to my position in VICI. This makes it my 21st-biggest holding, comprising 1.5% of my portfolio. In a few weeks, I anticipate upping my position by another 20% to 25%. That's why I'm sticking with my Buy rating.

data.title: EQT Corporation: Natural Gas Prices Unchained
data.description:
Introduction

The start of the supply chain, or picks and shovels of the AI and data center buildout, is natural gas, which is powering electricity generation (nuclear and uranium are a bit further out, 2030). I have covered key IPPs (independent power producers), the turbine makers, and a host of core data center suppliers, such as semiconductor storage, servers, Ethernet, cooling, and cable suppliers. In August, I dove into the US natural gas sector and came away with two buys, EQT Corporation (EQT) and Expand (EXE), formerly Chesapeake Energy.

Natural Gas Demand

The demand for natural gas is being driven by electricity generation (gas turbines) to feed data centers, warmer summers, and colder winters, as well as LNG exports. The combination, according to Bloomberg, Wood Mackenzie, and JPMorgan, could lead to a tight supply/demand balance that pushes natural gas prices upwards towards the global levels.

The US has benefited from an abundant production of cheap natural gas, with the benchmark prices (HH, or Henry Hub) hovering in the US$2/BTU range for years. The producers developed highly efficient operations and extensive pipeline networks to get the gas to market and saturate consumers, i.e., we are dependent on this fuel source. The rise in LNG, which freezes and compresses the gas for long-distance transport, began to liberate production for far higher-priced export markets. This trend alone would have led to a breakout from the HH US$2/BTU levels. The electricity demand from AI data centers compounds the demand side and should drive prices higher and faster.

Nuclear-powered electricity is rising with the start-up of various dormant plants, but a significant contribution is not likely until beyond 2030, led by the SMR (small modular reactors) technology. While sun and wind have been curtailed by the current administration, they exacerbate the imbalances.

The EQT model

My EQT estimates assume that natural gas prices per cubic foot average US$3.8 in 2025 or about 25% above 2025. This takes into account EQT's hedging strategies and a tight market where demand outpaces supply and storage. A harsher winter or steaming summer can add further upside. For 2027 and 2028 I assume a 5% annual increase. The larger price gains, into the US$8 level, may occur closer to 2030 when the data center and LNG exports result in structural natural gas deficits.

On the cost side, I have a more conservative estimate with the average cost per cubic foot increasing 1% per year vs. material reduction on scale gains, M&A synergies. This results in EBITDA forecasts that are in line with consensus for 2026 and begin to diverge in 2027 and become larger in 2028.

Note that the LNG trading deal should begin in 2030 when the various plants come online. The MVP pipeline is slated to start operation in mid-2028 and is also not included in the estimates.

Financial Estimates

The higher natural gas prices with flattish capex could lead to increasing FCF of over US$4bn, which should result in debt reduction and higher share buybacks. My preferred growth metric is cash earnings (net income, depreciation, and stock-based compensation), may grow over 20% on average in the 2026-2028 periods. EQT will probably acquire more production assets and distribution as gas prices climb and deficits intensify.

Peer Comps

I gathered consensus data on producers with at least 30% of revenue derived from natural gas. EQT's consensus estimates are valued at 6.3x P/CE (cash earnings), in line with the peers, but one of the cheapest on a relative or PEG ratio at 0.36x. As with the oil sector, valuations have been de-rated given the weak commodity pricing and seasonal volatility. I would argue that this view should change due to a tighter market and increasing prices. LNG exports are key to leveling US natural gas prices with the rest of the world.

Valuation

I am raising my price target to US$73 from US$61 due to higher estimates and an increase in the target multiple to 7x vs 6x. As discussed in the peer comp section, I believe this sector and especially EQT should garner higher valuations as natural gas prices increase and supply deficits become more normal. While seasonal volatility will persist, data center lead electricity needs and LNG exports, plus greater storage capacity, may reduce price volatility and improve investor confidence.

Risk

While EQT is not a direct AI story, the gas to run the turbines to make the electricity that the data center needs could face issues, and about 30% of new gas demand may not materialize, leading to a drop in prices. The LNG export prices may suffer in an overcapacity situation as other parts of the world build LNG plants, such as in Argentina. Finally, EQT and the sector may face regulatory and environmental headwinds with new administrations that favor renewable energy.

Conclusion

I rate EQT a buy. US natural gas has entered a period of structural deficits with the ability to be exported via LNG, while generation demand for data centers adds further to the demand picture. EQT could see sustained 20% earnings growth as natural gas prices climb toward global levels.

data.title: Why The Next 12-18 Months Are Crucial For IREN's Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

The present article explains why the next 12–18 months are crucial for IREN Limited’s (IREN) performance. In my view, IREN’s stock is suitable only for investors with a high risk tolerance, given its high-risk, high-reward profile.

My long-term view on the company is bullish. The paradox is that the market already values the company as an AI infrastructure provider, while most of its current revenue is still generated from its mining operations, does that right? In short yes.

Therefore, IREN’s valuation currently is somewhere between a mining and an AI-related company. I believe investors should view IREN strictly as an AI player—not only because of its deal with Microsoft (MSFT), but also due to its moat in low-cost power consumption and its already-available capacity for large-scale AI workload.

IREN Is Mining or AI Player?

The bitcoin mining business accounts for approximately 97% of total revenue, generating $232M in Q1’26. In total, IREN reported $240M in revenue, representing an impressive 355% YoY and 33% QoQ growth.

IREN announced a 5-year contract with MSFT with a total contract value ((TCV)) of $9.7B, and is expected to generate $1.94B in annual recurring revenue (ARR). Therefore, in the next couple of years—as IREN builds and delivers MSFT’s data center—the company will generate increasingly more revenue from its AI Cloud Services compared to its mining business.

If we annualize the current $240M of revenue, we reach roughly $1B in revenue for FY26. But IREN anticipates $1.9B in ARR from the Microsoft deal—indicating potential revenue growth of at least 90% in the following years. the company is transitioning from a mining business to an AI player, and investors should no longer evaluate it as a mining company despite the (still) low contribution of AI cloud revenue today.

Therefore, in my view, investors should value the company as an AI infrastructure company — keep this point in mind, as we will need it when assessing its valuation.

The Following 12-18 Months Are Crucial

I believe that the following mid-term window is the most important for both the company and its stock performance. Before explaining why, it’s important to note that the company has secured 3GW of data center capacity across multiple sites, while only 460MW—or just 16%—is currently in use. This highlights the company’s huge expansion potential.

Also, based on the company’s statement:

So as I alluded to earlier, a key driver of IREN's competitive advantage in AI cloud services is our vertical integration. We develop our own greenfield sites, engineer our own high-voltage infrastructure, build and operate our own data centers and deploy our own GPUs. Simply put, we control the entire stack from the substation all the way down to the GPU…This model of vertical integration eliminates dependence on third-party colocation providers and most importantly, removes all counterparty risk associated.

Its moat relies on this vertically integrated model, something that MSFT itself highlighted, and in my view was one of the key reasons behind this deal.

Now let me explain the factors behind why I believe the following months are crucial.

  1. First and most obvious is that IREN has to deliver in time the deployment phases related to the Microsoft deal through 2026. Although, the Co-Founder’s statement:

…we have never ever missed a construction or commissioning date in our life as a listed company” is a positive signal, this deal is the first really big and point in the company among AI infrastructure players -like Nebius (NBIS). In my view, if achieve to “meet the contract” will be a significant milestone which easy could attract new customers and hyperscalers.

2. Secondly, in combination with the first factor, the company currently uses only 16% of its total power capacity. IREN has already invested heavily to build or transition its mining operations into AI infrastructure sites. As shown in the table below, IREN’s CapEx is much higher than its operating cash flow (OCF), resulting in the company being a cash-burning business — meaning it simply generates negative free cash flow (FCF).

Notably, IREN plans to invest roughly $5.8B in CapEx for the Microsoft project, and to develop the previously mentioned 3GW capacity sites. The company aims to reach 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026, compared to 23,000 GPUs today. Microsoft’s 20% prepayment support IREN’s cash position and reduces the need for external financing, but it cannot fully cover the company’s total CapEx needs.

As mentioned:

Looking ahead, we're executing now on a plan that will see our GPU fleet scale from 23,000 GPUs today up to 140,000 GPUs by the end of 2026. When fully deployed, this expansion is expected to support in the order of $3.4 billion in annualized run rate revenue. Importantly, this expansion leverages just 16% of our 3 gigawatts in secured power, leaving ample capacity for future expansion.

It’s obvious that if the company achieve to secure contracts for its full 3GW capacity, then the projected $3.4B in ARR is a realistic target. My risk concern is that the company first develops its AI infrastructure and then signing client deals. To give a complete view of IREN’s future prospects, I believe that we should anticipate additional large contracts with different hyperscalers. I conclude the above after this based on management’s statement:

There is appetite from a number of parties in discussing cloud and other structures well above the 200 megawatts that’s been signed with Microsoft.

3. Reaching my final crucial factor, I want to highlight that the company holds a favorable electricity cost model. According to the company, the cost of electricity to operate its data centers is approximately $0.05/kWh, which is significantly below the industry average of $0.10/kWh.

The vertical integration, as mentioned earlier, and the low cost of electricity are, in my opinion, the company’s main moat catalysts. Investors should monitor whether these catalysts attract new customers and, most importantly, how efficiently the company take advantage of them.

My point, especially regarding electricity costs, is that they are not guaranteed that will stable forever. Currently, IREN offers an extremely low energy cost for its data centers, far below the industry average. Therefore, if the company cannot attract new customers within a reasonable time frame, this will be a negative future catalyst.

Bottom Line

I believe that IREN has huge potential but needs to demonstrate its capabilities to execute the step up (starting with the MSFT deal). My earlier concern remains: the high CapEx requirement combined with the fact that only 16% of its total capacity is currently contracted.

IREN has strong growth catalysts in its vertical integration and low energy costs, and it has already secured 3GW of capacity, signaling that it could become a major AI player. Investors should assess IREN as an AI infrastructure company rather than a mining company.

Moreover, IREN was unprofitable in Q1’26 in terms of operating income. I choose operating income as my key valuation metric because IREN holds financial instruments (specifically Capped Calls) that benefit its reported profitability, which may not fully reflect the company’s real operational performance.

Nowadays, IREN has a market cap of $13B, while its total revenue is roughly $1B annualized, as mentioned earlier. The Price/Operating Income (P/OI) is negative, reflecting an unprofitable company, while the Price/Sales ratio of 15.81 is far above the sector median of 3.55.

Stock seems overvalued and already priced with the MSFT's deal news. However, in my view, the current stock price does not fully reflect the company’s potential. The three crucial factors discussed above, along with its risks, contribute to its high-risk, high-reward profile.

Overall, I rate this stock as BUY, but I suggest it only for investors with high risk tolerance and only as a small portion of their portfolios. I believe that the company’s ability to “meet the MSFT contract,” combined with vertical integration and low energy costs, positions it well to attract new customers.

Therefore, if you are a long-term investor with durability for risk, IREN’s stock is a BUY.

data.title: Real Income Continues To Rise
data.description:
Real Personal Income continues to rise as Light Weight Vehicle SAAR is reduced.

Light Weight Vehicle sales continue sluggish primarily due to Toyota’s (TM) keeping dealer inventories low. Toyota remains the #2 brand in the US market, and the company continues to manufacture vehicles at a pace that is well below demand. Toyota recently had all top 10 slots on the list of fewest days in inventory, an average in the low 30 days of inventory range, while other brands’ vehicles have ranged as high as sitting on lots more than 1yr.

Buyers have been both brand-loyal and sensitive to price as many dealerships loaded vehicles with high-cost options, pushing vehicle costs out of range of many. Toyota, reportedly, has not done that.

There are no signs of pending recession in this data.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.
